result:
I want to write about a small, underrated human miracle: the way we manage to live inside time without being crushed by it.

Time is a brute fact, and yet we treat it like a medium we can swim through. We lend it shapes—weeks, semesters, quarters, “seasons of life”—and then we behave as if those shapes are real structures rather than chalk outlines we drew on the floor. We say we’re “behind,” as if the present were a moving sidewalk and everyone else were gliding forward at a steady pace. We say we’re “ahead,” as if there were a finish line and we had arrived early enough to lounge near it. We talk about “saving time,” which makes time sound like money, and we talk about “spending time,” which makes it sound like fuel, and we talk about “wasting time,” which makes it sound like trash—something you can throw away and still keep the rest.

But the strange thing is that these metaphors work. Not perfectly, not always, but often enough that a whole civilization can coordinate on them. A meeting happens at 2 p.m. instead of whenever the sun is highest. A plane takes off on schedule. A project runs late. A child’s birthday arrives with the emotional force of a verdict. The metaphors become practical. They become habits. They become feelings in the body: the subtle tension you get when you know a deadline is near, the anticipatory nausea of “soon,” the lightness of “later.”

What’s fascinating is that time is not just the thing we measure; it’s also the thing that measures us. It places our lives into units whether we like it or not. Childhood ends. A decade passes. A friendship fades or deepens. A person you love changes so slowly you can’t see it until one day you suddenly can, like realizing a tree has become enormous only after you remember what it looked like when it was planted. We don’t just travel through time; time travels through us, shaping our memories, our expectations, our capacity for hope and regret.

And memory—memory is where time becomes personal.

A clock doesn’t remember. A calendar doesn’t mourn. But you do. You carry moments not as timestamps but as textures. The smell of a certain soap in a certain bathroom when you were eight. The exact color of the sky during a conversation that went badly. The way the air felt the first time you walked into a hospital for a reason that wasn’t routine. These are not organized like spreadsheets. They’re organized like weather. When they return, they come with pressure systems: an old embarrassment can make your cheeks burn years later, as if the body is incapable of understanding that the event is over. A good memory can warm you, like stepping into sun after shade, even if the memory is of something objectively small.

If time were purely linear, memory would just be an archive. But it’s not. Memory is a kind of time travel with terrible navigation. You can’t choose your destination reliably. You can’t always stay there. Sometimes you don’t want to go at all, and yet there you are: suddenly in a room from ten years ago, with your past self standing where you left them, holding the same thoughts like a set of keys. You’re older, you’re supposed to know better, and yet something in you still believes the old story.

This makes living an act of translation. We translate past into present. We translate feelings from one era into another. We translate pain into caution, joy into standards, disappointment into cynicism or into wisdom depending on what we can afford. A great deal of what we call “maturity” is just improved translation: learning which feelings are old currency and which still spend.

The question that keeps appearing, in different disguises, is how to be in the present without being eaten by it.

We romanticize presence. We talk about mindfulness as if it were a lamp you can switch on. But presence is hard. The present is where uncertainty lives, where the future hasn’t agreed to behave, where your efforts might fail and you won’t know for a while. The present is also where the past can’t be fixed. You can revisit it, reinterpret it, forgive it, try to learn from it—but you can’t go back and change what happened, and some part of you will always want to. The present is the only place you can act, but it is also the only place where action feels risky.

So we escape.

We escape into the past as nostalgia: a curated museum of ourselves, where the exhibits are well lit and nothing is actually happening. We escape into the future as fantasy: the place where everything will be resolved, where the version of you who “has it together” resides like a distant cousin who always knows what to say. We escape into busyness, which is just the future disguised as the present. We escape into entertainment, which is a way of passing time while pretending we’re not. We escape into outrage, which is a highly stimulating form of time travel: you can be furious about something that happened years ago, in another place, to someone you’ll never meet, and for a while your own life becomes irrelevant.

Not all escape is bad. Sometimes you need relief. Sometimes you need to step outside yourself to survive yourself. The problem is when escape becomes your primary relationship with time. Then you stop living and start managing. You become like a person in an airport who is always between flights: technically traveling, but never arriving.

What would it mean to arrive?

Perhaps arriving is simply consenting to the fact that today exists. Not in a grand, mystical sense, but in the practical sense of inhabiting the day you have. Eating your breakfast as if breakfast is a real event. Answering one email without simultaneously planning the next ten. Feeling a feeling without instantly converting it into a strategy. Letting yourself be bored long enough for your mind to stop demanding novelty like a spoiled animal.

Boredom is interesting here because boredom is time becoming visible. When you’re bored, you feel the minutes. They stretch. They become heavy. Boredom is the sensation of being trapped in the present without adequate distraction. That sounds terrible, and it can be, but boredom is also an opening. It’s a crack in the constant flow of inputs. It’s a chance for the mind to wander without being harnessed to consumption. Many people report that their best ideas arrive not during frantic activity but during moments of boredom: showering, walking, waiting. These are the times when the mind is allowed to assemble itself.

Modern life is designed to eliminate these times. We carry devices that can fill every gap. We stand in line and scroll. We sit at a red light and glance down. We feel the first hint of boredom and treat it like a problem to solve. The result is that time becomes smoother, but we become less able to tolerate its texture. We become less able to be alone with ourselves.

There’s a subtle irony: the more we try to optimize our time, the less time we seem to have.

This is not just a feeling. When you pack your schedule, you create a life with no margin. And margin is where life happens. Margin is where you notice things. Margin is where you can respond rather than react. Margin is where you can be generous—because generosity often takes time, and if you have none, you will become stingy not just with money but with attention, patience, and care. Margin is where you can change your mind. Without margin, you can’t afford to rethink anything, so you cling to whatever plan you made when you were less informed.

A life without margin feels efficient, but it is fragile. One unexpected event—a sick child, a broken car, a bout of depression, a new opportunity—can collapse the whole structure. People who live without margin often feel like they are failing even when they are doing everything “right,” because the system they’ve built has no tolerance for being human.

Being human includes needing rest, needing connection, needing play, needing time to grieve, needing time to recover from mistakes. It includes having days when your brain is slow, when your heart is heavy, when your willpower is gone. If your schedule assumes you are a machine, you will always feel behind, because you will always be catching up to a fantasy version of yourself who never gets tired.

We rarely say this plainly: productivity culture is, in part, a denial of mortality. It says: if you manage your time perfectly, you can outrun the fact that you will die. You can compress your life into output and then treat output as proof that your life mattered. I’m not against effort or achievement. I’m against the quiet terror that often powers them.

Mortality is not just an end; it’s a lens. It makes some things sharp and others blur. When you realize you don’t have infinite time, you start to care differently. The number of times you will see a friend is finite. The number of dinners you will eat with your parents is finite. The number of summers you will feel with the particular body you have right now is finite. That knowledge can make you anxious, but it can also make you tender. It can make you choose.

Choosing is where time becomes moral.

Every choice is a small act of allegiance. When you choose one thing, you are choosing not to do something else. That’s obvious, but it’s also easy to avoid thinking about. We distract ourselves from the cost of our choices by pretending we can do everything. We make lists that exceed the day, the week, the year. We overpromise because it feels good to be wanted. We keep options open because closing them feels like loss. But a life is made not by the options you had, but by the options you closed.

This is why “no” is sacred. Not because it’s empowering in some abstract self-help way, but because it protects the time where your life actually happens. If you say yes to everything, your time becomes a commons where anyone can graze. You become available but not present. You become helpful but not whole. You become a person who is everywhere and nowhere, like mist.

At the same time, too many noes can make your life small. There’s a kind of fear-based minimalism that looks like wisdom but is really avoidance. You can protect your time so aggressively that nothing new enters. You can curate your life until it is perfectly safe and perfectly stagnant. The art is in balancing the openness that allows life to surprise you with the boundaries that keep you from being consumed.

Surprise is another way time reveals itself. You can’t schedule surprise. You can prepare for it—by keeping margin, by staying curious, by meeting people—but you can’t command it. The best conversations often happen when you didn’t plan them. The most meaningful experiences sometimes arrive disguised as inconvenience. You take a wrong turn and discover a street you love. You miss a train and end up talking to someone you needed to meet. Not always, of course. Sometimes a wrong turn is just a wrong turn. The point isn’t to romanticize randomness; it’s to acknowledge that control is not the only path to a good life.

Control is alluring because it promises to make time predictable. If you can control enough variables, you can know what next year will look like. But life resists that. You can’t fully control health, economy, other people, your own mind. You can influence, you can prepare, you can reduce risk, but the future remains partly wild. This is not a flaw; it’s a condition.

And because the future is wild, we need rituals.

Rituals are how we domesticate time without pretending to own it. A morning routine, a weekly call, a yearly trip, a birthday cake—these things are not efficient. They are repeated for the sake of repetition. They create landmarks. They give the year a shape. They remind us that we are still here. They create continuity when everything else changes.

Ritual is often misunderstood as superstition, but it can be profoundly rational. If you know the world will change, you build something that stays. Not forever, not rigidly, but reliably enough that your nervous system can relax. A ritual is a promise you can keep even when other promises break.

This makes me think about how different people experience the passage of time. Children live in a thick present. A week is forever. An hour is an ocean. The future is an idea, and the past is a blurry story told by adults. Then adolescence arrives and time becomes dramatic: everything matters, everything is urgent, everything feels like it will define you permanently. In young adulthood, time becomes a resource. You think in terms of trajectories. You compare. You optimize. You worry. Later, if you are lucky, time becomes something else: not just a resource, but a companion. You begin to understand that you don’t get extra points for speed. You begin to see that the point was not to finish quickly, because there is no finishing. There is only living until you don’t.

Of course, this is not a neat progression, and it doesn’t happen for everyone. Some people grow older and become more frantic, not less. Some people become calm early. Some people never get the chance to make peace with time because life is too hard—because poverty, illness, discrimination, or trauma keeps them in survival mode. It’s important to say this because a lot of advice about time is advice from people who have it. When your life is crowded by necessity, “slow down” can sound like a joke.

Still, even in difficult circumstances, people find ways to make time humane. They sing while they work. They cook meals that take longer than they strictly need to. They tell stories. They sit on stoops. They make games out of boredom. They build community, which is maybe the most important technology for surviving time: having other people who remember you, who witness you, who hold pieces of your story when you can’t.

Community also changes time because it multiplies it. When you’re alone, your time can feel like a small jar: whatever you want must fit inside it. With others, time becomes shared. You can do things together that would be difficult alone. You can take turns being strong. You can borrow hope. You can share chores. You can create events. You can create a sense of “we” that stretches beyond your individual lifespan. This is why traditions matter: they’re time shared across generations.

But community has its own time problems. People are not synchronized. One person is ready for closeness when another is exhausted. One person wants change when another wants stability. Relationships require time, and time is always in dispute: how much, when, in what form. Many conflicts are secretly conflicts about time. “You never make time for me.” “I need space.” “I’m busy.” “I feel neglected.” “I feel smothered.” These are not just complaints; they are competing maps of how life should be lived.

To love someone is partly to negotiate time with them. It’s to create regularity without turning the relationship into an obligation. It’s to show up often enough that trust can form. Trust requires repetition. You can’t trust someone you see once a year unless the relationship is unusually robust or unusually simple. Trust is built in the small, unglamorous units of time: the check-ins, the errands, the mundane conversations about nothing. People say they want a deep relationship, but depth is usually a byproduct of accumulation. You don’t dive straight down; you keep showing up until the surface becomes familiar enough to risk depth.

This brings us to attention, which is the real currency of time. Not money. Not productivity. Attention.

You can be alive for eighty years and scarcely live if your attention is never yours. You can have your attention stolen by advertisements, by anxieties, by devices engineered to be irresistible, by work that never ends, by internal narratives that loop like broken songs. In that case, your time will be full but not owned. It will feel like renting a room in your own life.

Recovering attention is not about becoming a hermit or rejecting technology. It’s about reclaiming the right to decide what matters. It’s about training yourself to notice when your attention is being dragged. It’s about practicing the ability to stay with something that is not immediately rewarding: a book, a conversation, a craft, a difficult emotion, a long-term goal. Attention is like a muscle, and modern life weakens it in predictable ways. The good news is that muscles can be strengthened. The bad news is that strengthening them feels like work precisely because you are asking your mind to do something it has been trained not to do.

And yet, the payoff is enormous: a life that feels longer.

Not longer in the sense of more years, but longer in the sense of more lived moments. When you pay attention, the day expands. When you rush, the day disappears. When you scroll, you forget. When you walk and look around, you remember. This is why vacations feel long while you’re in them and short when you look back: novelty and attention create memory, and memory creates the sense of a full life. The days you can’t remember might as well not have happened, at least in terms of your felt experience.

So one practical way to “have more time” is to have more memory, and one way to have more memory is to pay more attention. This is not a trick; it’s a fundamental feature of the mind. We remember what we notice. We notice what we care about. We care about what we choose, and sometimes what chooses us.

There’s another layer: the story you tell yourself about time.

Some people live with a story of scarcity: there’s never enough time, I’m always late, everything is slipping away. Others live with a story of abundance: there’s time for what matters, life unfolds, I can adjust. Neither story is fully accurate. Time is both scarce and abundant depending on your circumstances and your expectations. But the story matters because it shapes how you move. If you believe time is always attacking you, you will become defensive. You will rush, hoard, resent. If you believe time is always generous, you may become careless. The best story might be one of stewardship: time is limited, so treat it with respect; time is offered daily, so treat it with gratitude; time cannot be controlled, so treat it with humility.

Humility toward time is underrated. It means recognizing that you will not get everything done. It means building a life where incompletion is not a moral failure. It means understanding that the list will regenerate like a hydra and that your job is not to slay it but to decide which heads matter today. It means accepting seasons: times to push, times to rest, times to rebuild. It means letting some dreams die so that others can live.

Letting dreams die is painful, but it can be freeing. There is a particular kind of adult grief that comes from realizing you cannot be all the versions of yourself you once imagined. You cannot be the musician and the surgeon and the novelist and the globe-trotting photographer and the perfect parent and the flawless friend and the person who never needs help. Something has to give. You will choose, or life will choose for you.

If you choose deliberately, you can mourn what you release and still feel integrity. If life chooses for you through drift, you may feel haunted by unlived lives. This is why deliberate choice matters—not because it guarantees happiness, but because it reduces regret. Regret often comes from not choosing, from letting time decide while pretending you had control.

I realize this is all abstract, so let’s make it concrete. Imagine a single ordinary day. You wake up. There is a moment, before the phone, before the mind grabs its usual worries, when the day is blank. Not empty—blank. It hasn’t been written yet. That moment is a kind of power. It’s also fragile. It disappears fast. But if you can protect it, even for thirty seconds, you can set a tone. You can ask: what would make this day feel honest? Not impressive. Not optimized. Honest.

Honest might mean: I need to apologize to someone. Honest might mean: I need to stop pretending I can handle this alone. Honest might mean: I need to work for two hours with full focus and then rest. Honest might mean: I need to go outside. Honest might mean: I need to let myself feel sad without trying to fix it immediately.

An honest day is not always a productive day. Sometimes honesty means admitting you’re depleted. Sometimes it means doing the boring, necessary thing you’ve avoided. Sometimes it means saying no. Sometimes it means saying yes to something that scares you. Time is the medium, but honesty is the practice.

At the end of that day, the day becomes a memory. It becomes part of the story you are building about your life. The story will not include everything. It will include what you noticed, what you felt, what you did that mattered to you. Over years, those stories accumulate into identity. You become, in part, what you repeatedly pay attention to. You become what you repeatedly do. You become what you repeatedly avoid. Time is not just passing; it is constructing you.

That’s the miracle and the burden: you are made of days.

And the days are made of moments so small they hardly seem like anything. A glance. A text. A breath. A decision to be kind when you could have been sharp. A decision to rest when you could have kept going. A decision to listen when you could have talked. These are not grand achievements. They are the basic units of a life that feels like it belongs to you.

If I had to reduce all of this to a simple wish, it would be this: that we would treat our time less like a battlefield and more like a garden.

A battlefield mindset turns every hour into a fight: against laziness, against distraction, against other people, against aging, against uncertainty. A garden mindset accepts cycles. It recognizes that growth is not linear. It recognizes that some days are for planting, some for watering, some for weeding, some for harvesting, and some for leaving the soil alone. It recognizes that you can’t force a seed to sprout by yelling at it. You can only create conditions.

Conditions matter. The conditions of your time—your environment, your relationships, your habits, your work demands—shape what is possible. You can change some conditions. You can’t change all. Wisdom is knowing the difference, and courage is acting where you can.

Time will keep moving. That is not negotiable. But how it feels, what it becomes, what it makes of you—that is partly up to your attention, your choices, your rituals, your margins, your loves.

We don’t get to stop time. We get to meet it.

And meeting it, even imperfectly, even briefly, is what makes a life.

I want to write about a small, underrated human miracle: the way we manage to live inside time without being crushed by it.

Time is a brute fact, and yet we treat it like a medium we can swim through. We lend it shapes—weeks, semesters, quarters, “seasons of life”—and then we behave as if those shapes are real structures rather than chalk outlines we drew on the floor. They become feelings in the body: the subtle tension you get when you know a deadline is near, the anticipatory nausea of “soon,” the lightness of “later.”

What’s fascinating is that time is not just the thing we measure; it’s also the thing that measures us. It places our lives into units whether we like it or not. Childhood ends. A decade passes. We escape into outrage, which is a highly stimulating form of time travel: you can be furious about something that happened years ago, in another place, to someone you’ll never meet, and for a while your own life becomes irrelevant.

Then adolescence arrives and time becomes dramatic: everything matters, everything is urgent, everything feels like it will define you permanently. In young adulthood, time becomes a resource. You think in terms of trajectories. You compare. You optimize. You worry. Later, if you are lucky, time becomes something else: not just a resource, but a companion. You begin to understand that you don’t get extra points for speed. You begin to see that the point was not to finish quickly, because there is no finishing. There is only living until you don’t.

Of course, this is not a neat progression, and it doesn’t happen for everyone. Some people grow older and become more frantic, not less. Some people become calm early. Some people never get the chance to make peace with time because life is too hard—because poverty, illness, discrimination, or trauma keeps them in survival mode. It’s important to say this because a lot of advice about time is advice from people who have it. When your life is crowded by necessity, “slow down” can sound like a joke.

Still, even in difficult circumstances, people find ways to make time humane. They sing while they work. They cook meals that take longer than they strictly need to. They tell stories. They sit on stoops. They make games out of boredom. They build community, which is maybe the most important technology for surviving time: having other people who remember you, who witness you, who hold pieces of your story when you can’t.

Community also changes time because it multiplies it. When you’re alone, your time can feel like a small jar: whatever you want must fit inside it. With others, time becomes shared. You can do things together that would be difficult alone. You can take turns being strong. You can borrow hope. You can share chores. You can create events. You can create a sense of “we” that stretches beyond your individual lifespan. This is why traditions matter: they’re time shared across generations.

But community has its own time problems. People are not synchronized. One person is ready for closeness when another is exhausted. One person wants change when another wants stability. Relationships require time, and time is always in dispute: how much, when, in what form. Many conflicts are secretly conflicts about time. “You never make time for me.” “I need space.” “I’m busy.” “I feel neglected.” “I feel smothered.” These are not just complaints; they are competing maps of how life should be lived.

To love someone is partly to negotiate time with them. It’s to create regularity without turning the relationship into an obligation. It’s to show up often enough that trust can form. Trust requires repetition. You can’t trust someone you see once a year unless the relationship is unusually robust or unusually simple. Trust is built in the small, unglamorous units of time: the check-ins, the errands, the mundane conversations about nothing. People say they want a deep relationship, but depth is usually a byproduct of accumulation. You don’t dive straight down; you keep showing up until the surface becomes familiar enough to risk depth.

This brings us to attention, which is the real currency of time. Not money. Not productivity. Attention.

You can be alive for eighty years and scarcely live if your attention is never yours. You can have your attention stolen by advertisements, by anxieties, by devices engineered to be irresistible, by work that never ends, by internal narratives that loop like broken songs. In that case, your time will be full but not owned. It will feel like renting a room in your own life.

Recovering attention is not about becoming a hermit or rejecting technology. It’s about reclaiming the right to decide what matters. It’s about training yourself to notice when your attention is being dragged. It’s about practicing the ability to stay with something that is not immediately rewarding: a book, a conversation, a craft, a difficult emotion, a long-term goal. Attention is like a muscle, and modern life weakens it in predictable ways. The good news is that muscles can be strengthened. The bad news is that strengthening them feels like work precisely because you are asking your mind to do something it has been trained not to do.

And yet, the payoff is enormous: a life that feels longer.

Not longer in the sense of more years, but longer in the sense of more lived moments. When you pay attention, the day expands. When you rush, the day disappears. When you scroll, you forget. When you walk and look around, you remember. This is why vacations feel long while you’re in them and short when you look back: novelty and attention create memory, and memory creates the sense of a full life. The days you can’t remember might as well not have happened, at least in terms of your felt experience.

So one practical way to “have more time” is to have more memory, and one way to have more memory is to pay more attention. This is not a trick; it’s a fundamental feature of the mind. We remember what we notice. We notice what we care about. We care about what we choose, and sometimes what chooses us.

There’s another layer: the story you tell yourself about time.

Some people live with a story of scarcity: there’s never enough time, I’m always late, everything is slipping away. Others live with a story of abundance: there’s time for what matters, life unfolds, I can adjust. Neither story is fully accurate. Time is both scarce and abundant depending on your circumstances and your expectations. But the story matters because it shapes how you move. If you believe time is always attacking you, you will become defensive. You will rush, hoard, resent. If you believe time is always generous, you may become careless. The best story might be one of stewardship: time is limited, so treat it with respect; time is offered daily, so treat it with gratitude; time cannot be controlled, so treat it with humility.

Humility toward time is underrated. It means recognizing that you will not get everything done. It means building a life where incompletion is not a moral failure. It means understanding that the list will regenerate like a hydra and that your job is not to slay it but to decide which heads matter today. It means accepting seasons: times to push, times to rest, times to rebuild. It means letting some dreams die so that others can live.

Letting dreams die is painful, but it can be freeing. There is a particular kind of adult grief that comes from realizing you cannot be all the versions of yourself you once imagined. You cannot be the musician and the surgeon and the novelist and the globe-trotting photographer and the perfect parent and the flawless friend and the person who never needs help. Something has to give. You will choose, or life will choose for you.

If you choose deliberately, you can mourn what you release and still feel integrity. If life chooses for you through drift, you may feel haunted by unlived lives. This is why deliberate choice matters—not because it guarantees happiness, but because it reduces regret. Regret often comes from not choosing, from letting time decide while pretending you had control.

I realize this is all abstract, so let’s make it concrete. Imagine a single ordinary day. You wake up. There is a moment, before the phone, before the mind grabs its usual worries, when the day is blank. Not empty—blank. It hasn’t been written yet. That moment is a kind of power. It’s also fragile. It disappears fast. But if you can protect it, even for thirty seconds, you can set a tone. You can ask: what would make this day feel honest? Not impressive. Not optimized. Honest.

Honest might mean: I need to apologize to someone. Honest might mean: I need to stop pretending I can handle this alone. Honest might mean: I need to work for two hours with full focus and then rest. Honest might mean: I need to go outside. Honest might mean: I need to let myself feel sad without trying to fix it immediately.

An honest day is not always a productive day. Sometimes honesty means admitting you’re depleted. Sometimes it means doing the boring, necessary thing you’ve avoided. Sometimes it means saying no. Sometimes it means saying yes to something that scares you. Time is the medium, but honesty is the practice.

At the end of that day, the day becomes a memory. It becomes part of the story you are building about your life. The story will not include everything. It will include what you noticed, what you felt, what you did that mattered to you. Over years, those stories accumulate into identity. You become, in part, what you repeatedly pay attention to. You become what you repeatedly do. You become what you repeatedly avoid. Time is not just passing; it is constructing you.

That’s the miracle and the burden: you are made of days.

And the days are made of moments so small they hardly seem like anything. A glance. A text. A breath. A decision to be kind when you could have been sharp. A decision to rest when you could have kept going. A decision to listen when you could have talked. These are not grand achievements. They are the basic units of a life that feels like it belongs to you.

If I had to reduce all of this to a simple wish, it would be this: that we would treat our time less like a battlefield and more like a garden.

A battlefield mindset turns every hour into a fight: against laziness, against distraction, against other people, against aging, against uncertainty. A garden mindset accepts cycles. It recognizes that growth is not linear. It recognizes that some days are for planting, some for watering, some for weeding, some for harvesting, and some for leaving the soil alone. It recognizes that you can’t force a seed to sprout by yelling at it. You can only create conditions.

Conditions matter. The conditions of your time—your environment, your relationships, your habits, your work demands—shape what is possible. You can change some conditions. You can’t change all. Wisdom is knowing the difference, and courage is acting where you can.

Time will keep moving. That is not negotiable. But how it feels, what it becomes, what it makes of you—that is partly up to your attention, your choices, your rituals, your margins, your loves.

We don’t get to stop time. We get to meet it.

And meeting it, even imperfectly, even briefly, is what makes a life.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
